items.title: Pick a tree only when order matters
items.avoid: Reaching for a BST for simple key-value access.
items.prefer: dict for O(1) by key; tree (or sorted container) for ordered/range queries.
items.why: Trees pay log-n cost for keyed lookup; only justify it when you need ordering, ranges, or in-order iteration.
items.example:
```python
from sortedcontainers import SortedDict
latency_by_ts = SortedDict()
latency_by_ts.irange(t0, t1)   # range query — what a dict can't do
```

items.title: Iterate, don't recurse, on deep trees
items.avoid: Recursive traversals on large data structures from untrusted sources.
items.prefer: Explicit stack/queue and an iterative loop.
items.why: Avoids RecursionError and lets you control memory.
items.example:
```python
def walk(root):
    stack = [root]
    while stack:
        n = stack.pop()
        if not n: continue
        yield n
        stack.append(n.right); stack.append(n.left)
```

items.title: Randomize inserts (or balance) to keep height bounded
items.avoid: Inserting sorted data into a vanilla BST.
items.prefer: Shuffle, batch-build from a sorted array (midpoint root), or use a self-balancing structure.
items.why: Keeps depth O(log n) and operations efficient.
items.example:
```python
def build_balanced(sorted_keys):
    if not sorted_keys: return None
    mid = len(sorted_keys) // 2
    return Node(sorted_keys[mid],
                build_balanced(sorted_keys[:mid]),
                build_balanced(sorted_keys[mid+1:]))
```

items.title: Validate BST invariants with in-order traversal
items.avoid: Trusting a BST is correct after a sequence of inserts/deletes.
items.prefer: In-order traversal should return strictly increasing keys.
items.why: Cheap sanity check; catches subtle insert/delete bugs.
items.example:
```python
seen = list(in_order(root))
assert all(a < b for a, b in zip(seen, seen[1:]))
```
